key_insight: Low-bit quantization can preserve answer accuracy while silently increasing reasoning length, creating a hidden compute cost through token inflation.
engineering_relevance: For serving quantized reasoning models, latency and cost should be measured on total reasoning tokens, not just per-token speed or final accuracy.
method: Defined CoT Token Inflation Ratio to compare reasoning length in quantized vs. full-precision models.
Evaluated INT4/INT3 post-training quantization across math reasoning, code generation, scientific QA, and agentic tool-use benchmarks.
Analyzed both output traces and end-to-end serving effects, and tested prompting, sampling, and quantization-aware training as mitigations.
evidence_signal: Across multiple benchmarks, INT4/INT3 models often kept accuracy but produced longer chains of thought, with more intermediate steps and semantic repetition.
watch_item: Accuracy-only reporting can miss real deployment regressions, and mitigation methods like prompting or decoding-time sampling showed inconsistent trade-offs.
paper_title: Quantization Inflates Reasoning: Token Inflation as a Hidden Cost of Low-Bit Reasoning Models
